channel.title: Syntax-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Syntax-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token
item.description:
Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token

This error occurs when Bash encounters a character or token it doesn’t expect while parsing a command or script.

What This Error Means

Bash follows strict syntax rules for command structure. When it encounters something out of place, it throws a parse error before execution begins.

Common Causes

  • Unclosed quotes
  • Misplaced parentheses or brackets
  • Special characters not escaped
  • Windows line endings (CRLF)

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check and close all quotes

# Wrong
echo "Hello world

# Right
echo "Hello world"

Fix 2: Escape special characters

# Wrong
echo Hello;World

# Right
echo "Hello;World"
echo Hello\;World

Fix 3: Convert Windows line endings

sed -i 's/\r$//' script.sh

Fix 4: Check parenthesis usage

# Wrong
var = (1 2 3)

# Right
var=(1 2 3)

Examples

$ echo "Hello
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `newline'

$ echo Hello )world
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `)'

Related Errors

  • Unmatched Quote - unexpected end of file due to unclosed quotes
  • Here Document Error - syntax issues with heredoc syntax

item.title: [Solution] Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token
item.description:
Bash Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token Fix

This error occurs when Bash encounters a character or token it doesn’t expect while parsing a command or script.

Description

Bash follows strict syntax rules for command structure. When it encounters something out of place — like an unclosed quote, misplaced parenthesis, or unexpected character — it throws a parse error before execution even begins.

Common Causes

  • Unclosed quotes — a missing closing " or ' makes Bash think the next token is part of the string.
  • Misplaced parentheses or brackets — using ( or ) in the wrong context.
  • Special characters not escaped — spaces, semicolons, or other metacharacters used literally without quoting.
  • Windows line endings (CRLF) — carriage return characters confuse the parser.

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check and close all quotes

# Wrong
echo "Hello world

# Right
echo "Hello world"

Fix 2: Escape special characters

# Wrong
echo Hello;World

# Right
echo "Hello;World"
# Or escape it
echo Hello\;World

Fix 3: Convert Windows line endings

sed -i 's/\r$//' script.sh

Fix 4: Check parenthesis usage

# Wrong — Bash thinks this is a subshell
var = (1 2 3)

# Right — use proper array syntax
var=(1 2 3)

Examples

$ echo "Hello
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `newline'

$ echo Hello )world
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `)'

$ ./script.sh
# script.sh has Windows line endings
bash: ./script.sh: line 1: $'\r': command not found

Related Errors

  • Unmatched Quote — unexpected end of file due to unclosed quotes.
  • Here Document Error — syntax issues with heredoc syntax.

item.title: [Solution] MATLAB Invalid Expression or Identifier Error Fix
item.description:
MATLAB Invalid Expression or Identifier Error Fix

This error occurs when MATLAB encounters a variable name, function name, or expression that violates its syntax rules. The message reads: Invalid expression. Check for missing operators or unbalanced delimiters. or Invalid MATLAB identifier.

Description

MATLAB requires identifiers (variable names, function names) to start with a letter, contain only letters, digits, or underscores, and not match reserved keywords. Expressions must have balanced parentheses, brackets, and braces. This error fires when these rules are violated — often due to typos, copy-paste artifacts, or improper use of operators.

item.title: [Solution] SQL Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
This error occurs when the SQL parser encounters a syntax error in the statement. The message reads: You have an error in your SQL syntax; check the manual that corresponds to your MySQL server version.

What This Error Means

The SQL statement has a grammatical error that prevents the parser from understanding it. This can be caused by missing keywords, misplaced commas, unmatched quotes, or incorrect clause ordering.

Common Causes

  • Missing comma between SELECT columns
  • Unmatched quotes or parentheses
  • Reserved keyword used as column name without backticks
  • Incorrect clause ordering (WHERE before FROM)
  • Missing closing parenthesis in subquery

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check for common syntax mistakes

-- Wrong: missing comma
SELECT name email FROM users;

-- Correct
SELECT name, email FROM users;

Fix 2: Quote reserved keywords

-- Wrong: "order" is a reserved keyword
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order = 1;

-- Correct: use backticks
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE `order` = 1;

Fix 3: Match parentheses

-- Wrong: unclosed parenthesis
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id FROM users;

-- Correct
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id FROM users) AS sub;

Fix 4: Check subquery syntax

-- Wrong: subquery must have alias
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id, name FROM users);

-- Correct
SELECT * FROM (SELECT id, name FROM users) AS user_list;

Examples

SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'Alice' AND;
-- ERROR 1064: You have an error in your SQL syntax near 'AND'

Related Errors

  • Unknown Column — column doesn’t exist
  • Table Not Found — table doesn’t exist

item.title: [Solution] SQL Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
SQL Syntax Error Fix

This error occurs when the database parser cannot interpret your SQL statement. The message reads: You have an error in your SQL syntax; check the manual that corresponds to your MySQL server version for the right syntax to use near '...'.

Description

SQL has strict grammar rules. The parser expects keywords, identifiers, and operators in specific positions. Any deviation — missing commas, misplaced quotes, wrong keyword order — triggers a syntax error before the query executes.

item.title: [Solution] SQL Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token Fix
item.description:
SQL Syntax Error Near Unexpected Token Fix

A SQL syntax error occurs when the SQL parser encounters a token it doesn’t expect at that position in the query.

What This Error Means

SQL has strict grammar rules. When the parser encounters an unexpected keyword, missing comma, wrong operator, or misplaced clause, it reports a syntax error with the position of the problematic token.

Common Causes

  • Missing comma between SELECT columns
  • Wrong keyword order (e.g., WHERE before FROM)
  • Unclosed quotes or parentheses
  • Using reserved words as identifiers without quoting
  • Trailing commas

How to Fix

1. Check comma placement

-- WRONG: Trailing comma
SELECT name, email, FROM users;

-- CORRECT: No trailing comma
SELECT name, email FROM users;

2. Use correct clause order

-- WRONG: Wrong order
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1 ORDER BY name GROUP BY status;

-- CORRECT: Proper clause order
SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE id = 1 GROUP BY status ORDER BY status;

3. Quote reserved words

-- WRONG: "order" is a reserved word
SELECT * FROM users ORDER order;

-- CORRECT: Quote it
SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY `order`;
-- Or rename the column
SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY order_number;

4. Close all parentheses and quotes

-- WRONG: Unclosed parenthesis
SELECT * FROM users WHERE (name = 'Alice';

-- CORRECT: Match all parentheses
SELECT * FROM users WHERE (name = 'Alice');

Related Errors

  • SQL Column Not Found — column missing
  • SQL Table Not Found — table missing
  • SQL Group By Error — grouping issues

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1002 — Unterminated string literal
item.description:
TS1002 — Unterminated string literal

TS1002 occurs when a string literal is not properly closed with a matching quote. This prevents TypeScript from parsing the rest of the file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing quote
const msg = "Hello, world; // TS1002

// Cause 2: Unescaped quote inside string
const quote = "She said "hello""; // TS1002

// Cause 3: String spanning lines without template literal
const multi = "Line 1
Line 2"; // TS1002

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add closing quote

const msg = "Hello, world";

Fix 2: Escape inner quotes

const quote = "She said \"hello\"";
// or use single quotes
const quote = 'She said "hello"';

Fix 3: Use template literals for multiline

const multi = `Line 1
Line 2`;

Related Errors

  • TS1361: Unterminated template literal — template literal variant.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — ';' expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a syntax error indicating TypeScript expected a semicolon at the specified position. Missing semicolons, malformed statements, or incorrect syntax trigger this error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after statement
let x = 5
let y = 10 // TS1005: ';' expected before 'let'

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
 console.log(i) // missing semicolon in some contexts

// Cause 3: Wrong syntax in import
import { name } from "./module"
import { other } from "./other" // may trigger TS1005

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in type definition
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

let x = 5;
let y = 10;

Fix 2: Check semicolon context

// Semicolons required after:
let x = 5;
const y = "hello";
function foo() { return 1; }

Fix 3: Enable auto-semicolons in editor

Configure your editor to insert semicolons automatically.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — Semicolon Expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected a semicolon. This usually means there’s a syntax issue with how statements, declarations, or expressions are structured.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after variable declaration
let x = 10
let y = 20 // TS1005 on this line — expected ';' after declaration

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
{ // TS1005 — expected ';' after for statement

// Cause 3: Missing semicolon after interface property
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005 if strict semicolon enforcement
}

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in import statement
import { helper } from "./utils"
import { other } from "./other" // TS1005

Solutions

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

// Wrong
let x = 10
let y = 20

// Correct
let x = 10;
let y = 20;

Fix 2: Use a linter to enforce semicolons

// .eslintrc.json
{
 "rules": {
 "semi": ["error", "always"]
 }
}

Fix 3: Check for missing closing brackets or braces

// Wrong — missing closing parenthesis
const result = add(1, 2
// TS1005: expected ';' — really means missing ')'

// Correct
const result = add(1, 2);

Fix 4: Fix malformed expressions

// Wrong — double operator
let x = 10 ++ 5; // TS1005

// Correct
let x = 10 + 5;

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally using a keyword
let class = "myClass"; // TS1005: ';' expected (class is reserved)

// Example 2: Forgetting semicolon in chained method
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name) // OK with ASI
 .join(", "); // TS1005 if context requires semicolon

// Fix: add semicolon at end
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name)
 .join(", ");

Related Errors

  • TS1109: Expression expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected token.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1009 — Declaration or statement expected
item.description:
TS1009 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1009 is a parser-level syntax error indicating TypeScript expected a declaration or statement at a position where it found something else. This is typically caused by malformed code structure.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra comma in declaration list
let x, y, , z; // TS1009

// Cause 2: Invalid token after import
import from "module"; // TS1009: missing import specifiers

// Cause 3: Unexpected token in interface
interface User {
 name: string,
 , // TS1009
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove extra commas

let x, y, z;

Fix 2: Fix import syntax

import { something } from "module";
// or
import defaultExport from "module";

Fix 3: Fix interface declaration

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

Related Errors

  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — similar parser error.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon expected.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a syntax error indicating TypeScript expected an expression at a position where none was found. This typically happens with malformed function calls, object literals, or template literals.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing argument in function call
doSomething(); // if function expects args and comma is missing
doSomething(, "hello"); // TS1109: expression expected before comma

// Cause 2: Empty template literal issue
const msg = `Hello, ${}`; // TS1109: expression expected inside ${}

// Cause 3: Invalid object syntax
const obj = {
 name: "Alice"
 age: 30 // TS1109 if comma missing
};

// Cause 4: Malformed ternary
const x = true ? ; // TS1109: expression expected after ?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check for missing expressions

// Remove extra commas
doSomething("hello");

// Fill in template literal
const msg = `Hello, ${name}`;

Fix 2: Add missing commas in objects

const obj = {
 name: "Alice",
 age: 30
};

Fix 3: Complete ternary expressions

const x = true ? "yes" : "no";

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — missing semicolon.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — missing declaration.
  • TS1136: Expected block of statements — missing block.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression Expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected an expression. This usually happens with malformed function calls, missing operands, or incomplete code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Empty function call argument
const result = Math.random() // Missing closing paren or argument

// Cause 2: Missing right operand
let x = 5 + ; // TS1109: expression expected after '+'

// Cause 3: Incomplete arrow function
const fn = () => // TS1109: expression expected after '=>'

// Cause 4: Missing value in assignment
const obj = {
 key: // TS1109: expression expected after ':'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Complete the expression

// Wrong
let x = 5 + ;

// Correct
let x = 5 + 10;

Fix 2: Close parentheses and provide arguments

// Wrong
const result = Math.random()

// Correct
const result = Math.random();

Fix 3: Provide a return value for arrow functions

// Wrong
const fn = () =>

// Correct
const fn = () => 42;

// Or with block body
const fn = () => {
 return 42;
};

Fix 4: Complete object property values

// Wrong
const obj = {
 key:
};

// Correct
const obj = {
 key: "value"
};

Examples

// Example 1: Incomplete template literal
const name = `Hello, ${; // TS1109

// Fix:
const name = `Hello, ${user.name}`;

// Example 2: Missing argument in new expression
const el = new HTMLElement(); // If HTMLElement isn't defined
// TS1109 if the expression can't be resolved

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader token error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters an unexpected token at a position where a declaration or statement is expected. This is a broad syntax error that can appear in many contexts.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Stray characters outside functions
const x = 5;
} // TS1128: stray closing brace

// Cause 2: Extra closing parenthesis
const arr = [1, 2, 3]); // TS1128

// Cause 3: Invalid top-level expression
5 + 3; // OK in some contexts, but in type file...

// Cause 4: Missing body after declaration
function foo(); // TS1128 if not in ambient declaration

How to Fix

Fix 1: Remove stray characters

const x = 5;
// no stray braces

Fix 2: Check bracket matching

const arr = [1, 2, 3]; // correct brackets

Fix 3: Use declare for ambient declarations

declare function foo(): void; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon expected.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.
  • TS1136: Expected block of statements — block expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or Statement Expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters a token where it expects a declaration (variable, function, class, etc.) or a statement. This often indicates a structural syntax problem like extra closing braces, misplaced code, or invalid top-level constructs.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra closing brace
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}} // TS1128: extra '}' — declaration or statement expected

// Cause 2: Code outside of a function or class at invalid location
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // TS1128: unexpected indentation / misplaced code

// Cause 3: Missing function body or expression
function compute() // TS1128: expected '{' or '=>'

// Cause 4: Invalid top-level expression
42 // TS1128 if not in a module or script context properly

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove extra closing braces

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}}

// Correct
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}

Fix 2: Ensure code is inside proper blocks

// Wrong
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // outside the function

// Correct
function process() {
 const x = 10; // inside the function
}

Fix 3: Complete function declarations

// Wrong
function compute()

// Correct
function compute(): number {
 return 42;
}

Fix 4: Check for misplaced imports or exports

// Wrong — export outside module context
export default {} // TS1128 if file is not a module

// Correct — ensure the file has at least one import/export
import { something } from "./other";
export default {};

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally closing a block early
if (condition) {
 doSomething();
} // Extra close
doOtherThing(); // TS1128

// Example 2: Mixing declaration and expression
const obj = {
 method() {}
}
const x = 10 // Missing semicolon, may cause TS1128 on next line

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — incomplete expressions.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1136 — Expected block of statements
item.description:
TS1136 — Expected block of statements

TS1136 occurs when TypeScript expects a block of statements (wrapped in {}) but finds something else instead. This typically happens with control structures missing their body braces.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing braces after if
if (condition)
 doSomething(); // TS1136 in some parser contexts

// Cause 2: Empty block
if (condition) {
 // TS1136 if body is expected but empty
}

// Cause 3: Wrong syntax for function body
function foo() // TS1136: expected block

How to Fix

Fix 1: Always use braces for blocks

if (condition) {
 doSomething();
}

Fix 2: Provide function body

function foo() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Use empty block explicitly

if (condition) {
 // intentionally empty
}

Related Errors

  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — expression expected.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — declaration expected.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1361 — Unterminated template literal
item.description:
TS1361 — Unterminated template literal

TS1361 occurs when a template literal (backtick string) is not properly closed with a matching backtick. This is a syntax error that prevents TypeScript from parsing the file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing backtick
const msg = `Hello, world; // TS1361

// Cause 2: Missing closing backtick after expression
const name = "Alice";
const greeting = `Hello, ${name}; // TS1361

// Cause 3: Unclosed multiline template
const html = `
 <div>
 <h1>Title</h1>
 </div> // TS1361 if closing backtick missing
`;

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add closing backtick

const msg = `Hello, world`;

Fix 2: Ensure expression blocks are complete

const greeting = `Hello, ${name}`;

Fix 3: Check multiline templates

const html = `
 <div>
 <h1>Title</h1>
 </div>
`;

Related Errors

  • TS1002: Unterminated string literal — regular string variant.
  • TS1005: ‘;’ expected — semicolon issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1434 — Unexpected Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1434 — Unexpected keyword or identifier

TS1434 occurs when TypeScript encounters a keyword or identifier in a position where it doesn’t belong according to the grammar rules. This is typically caused by using reserved words in invalid contexts or placing identifiers where expressions or declarations are expected.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a reserved keyword as an identifier
let class = "myClass"; // TS1434: Unexpected keyword 'class'

// Cause 2: Misplaced 'return' outside a function
return 42; // TS1434 if at top level

// Cause 3: Using 'type' as a variable name (pre-5.0)
type = 10; // TS1434: 'type' is a reserved keyword in TypeScript

// Cause 4: Keyword in wrong position in type annotation
type Foo = public string; // TS1434: 'public' not valid here

Solutions

Fix 1: Rename the identifier to avoid reserved words

// Wrong
let class = "myClass";

// Correct
let className = "myClass";

Fix 2: Move return statements inside functions

// Wrong
return 42;

// Correct
function getValue() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Use different variable names for reserved words

// Wrong — 'type' is reserved in TypeScript
let type = "foo";

// Correct
let typeValue = "foo";

Fix 4: Remove invalid modifiers from type annotations

// Wrong
type Foo = public string;

// Correct — access modifiers are for class members, not type aliases
type Foo = string;

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'const' in wrong context
interface Config {
 const host: string; // TS1434: 'const' not valid in interface
}

// Fix: remove 'const'
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

// Example 2: Using 'async' outside function
async const result = fetch("/api"); // TS1434

// Fix: use async function or async IIFE
const result = (async () => fetch("/api"))();

Related Errors

  • TS1435: Unknown keyword or identifier — unrecognized keyword.
  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1435 — Unknown Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1435 — Unknown keyword or identifier

TS1435 occurs when TypeScript encounters an identifier that it doesn’t recognize as a valid keyword or identifier in the current context. This often means a typo in a keyword, using a future/reserved word, or an unrecognized syntax.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in a keyword
const x = 10;
pritn(x); // TS1435: 'pritn' is not recognized (typo of 'print')

// Cause 2: Using a keyword from another language
def greet(): // TS1435: 'def' is not a TypeScript keyword (Python syntax)

// Cause 3: Unrecognized decorator or modifier
@sealed // TS1435 if decorator not recognized or not enabled
class MyClass {}

// Cause 4: Misspelled type annotation keyword
let x: numberr = 10; // TS1435: 'numberr' is not a valid type

Solutions

Fix 1: Correct keyword typos

// Wrong
pritn(x);

// Correct
console.log(x);

Fix 2: Use valid TypeScript keywords

// Wrong — Python syntax
def greet():

// Correct — TypeScript function
function greet(): void {}

Fix 3: Enable decorator support if needed

// tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "experimentalDecorators": true,
 "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
 }
}

Fix 4: Fix type annotation typos

// Wrong
let x: numberr = 10;

// Correct
let x: number = 10;

Examples

// Example 1: Using a non-existent type
let data: Arrray<number> = []; // TS1435: 'Arrray' not recognized

// Fix:
let data: Array<number> = [];

// Example 2: Wrong loop syntax from another language
for i in range(10) { // TS1435: not valid TypeScript
}

// Fix:
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {}

Related Errors

  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — keyword in wrong position.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.
